title: Phenotypic traits, genetic diversity, and toxigenic potential of Aspergillus flavus from maize ear rots in Sichuan Province, China.
author_string: Ma, Miaomiao, Zhao, Henan, Wang, Wei, Li, Pan, Yang, Rui et al.
abstract: Aspergillus flavus, a well-known producer of aflatoxin, causes maize ear rots, which compromises crop quality and threatens food security. Samples of maize ear rots were collected from 13 districts in Sichuan Province between 2017 and 2021. A total of 467 isolates were obtained, and 256 representative strains were selected for molecular identification through multi-locus phylogenetic analysis based on RPB2 and CaM sequences, and all isolates were identified to A. flavus. Inter-simple sequence repeat‌ (ISSR) analysis showed that the genetic diversity of A. flavus isolates was associated with geographical origin. All isolates caused maize ear rot, with a disease index ranging from 6.52 to 58.67. Additionally, 359 strains were L-type, producing sclerotia with an average diameter of 516.54 μm; 44 strains were S-type, with a diameter of 352.43 μm; and 64 isolates did not produce sclerotia, identified as N-type (none-sclerotia) strains. The capacity of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) production indicated that 270 strains (57.82 %) produce AFB1, with a wide range from 13.26 to 8160.01 μg/mL, while 197 strains (42.18 %) produced none. Further correlation analysis showed a significant positive correlation between the disease index and both sclerotia size and AFB1 production, although no correlation was found between sclerotia size and AFB1 production. Furthermore, we discovered that the optimal temperature for the growth and aflatoxin production of A. flavus was 28 °C, with the ideal water activity (a<sub>w</sub>) being 0.99 and 0.93 on maize-based substrate, respectively. This research systematically examined the etiologic and biological characteristics, as well as the population diversity of A. flavus in Sichuan for the first time, uncovering genetic differentiation among geographically distinct populations of A. flavus. The results provide insights for developing targeted management strategies against A. flavus and help define critical storage parameters to reduce the risk of AFB1 contamination in maize.
